Author: Phaniv Com
Title: Intent, Execution, Validation — The New SDLC for the AI Era
Text:
Intent, Execution, Validation — The New SDLC for the AI Era
Why My Best Engineers Now Write the Least Code
A year ago, when I reviewed my team’s work, I was evaluating code quality — structure, patterns, test coverage, and performance. Today, I’m evaluating the quality of their thinking. That single shift changes everything downstream.
It took me a while to articulate why. We adopted AI coding assistants enthusiastically. Velocity went up. But something subtler changed, too: the bottleneck moved. It was no longer ‘can the engineer write this?’ It became ‘Does the engineer understand the problem clearly enough to direct AI toward the right outcome?’
Programming is no longer the constraint; thinking is.
AI Agents are not just for development. The real leverage comes when AI is present across the entire SDLC — not injected at the implementation step, but woven through from the moment you start thinking about the problem.
This is the core idea I want to explore: a framework I call Intent, Execution, Validation — three layers that together represent a reimagined SDLC for a world where AI is the connective tissue, not just a tool.
The Problem: AI Entered at the Wrong Stage
Most teams today use AI Agents the same way — as an accelerator during implementation. You write requirements elsewhere. You design the solution elsewhere. You align on acceptance criteria elsewhere. Then you hand off to an AI Agent and ask it to write the code.
That’s a late injection. And it has a hidden cost.
Think of it through the lens of shift-left — the well-established practice of moving quality activities earlier in the pipeline. A defect found in requirements costs a fraction of what it costs to find in testing. The same principle applies to AI. Misaligned intent caught during story design costs almost nothing to fix. Misaligned intent caught during a PR review costs a sprint.
But there’s a second problem: context-switching tax. Engineers today use multiple tools — the IDE, JIRA, GitHub, Confluence, Slack — to complete a single piece of work. Each switch carries a cognitive cost. It breaks flow. And flow state is where the real output happens. We’ve made AI faster without making the workflow smarter.
The result is a landscape where AI is used in silos rather than as a continuous presence — leaving the friction of coordination and context-rebuilding stubbornly human.
The Framework: Intent, Execution, Validation
Over the past year of working with AI Agents extensively — for design discussions, story generation, implementation, and code review — I’ve come to see the SDLC through three distinct layers. Each layer has a different primary activity, a different human role, and a different AI contribution.
The insight is not that each layer benefits from AI individually — most teams already know that. The insight is that all three layers should be connected, with context flowing continuously from Intent through Execution into Validation, inside a single environment: the IDE
The Pilot: Building Without a Single Custom Command
Six months before automating this workflow, I stress-tested the philosophy by developing one of our most complex features using AI as the primary engine. No custom commands, no MCP integrations — just a principle applied deliberately.
My approach was Documentation as Code. I didn’t start with the IDE; I started with intent:
▸ Living Artifacts: Requirements, architectural designs, and technical translations lived directly in the repository — version-controlled alongside the code they informed, not buried in a wiki.
▸ The Feedback Loop: When AI output drifted, I didn’t fix the code manually. I pointed the AI back to the design documentation and asked it to self-correct against those constraints. Intent documented is intent enforceable.
▸ Validator-in-Chief: I fed AI-generated design specs back into the AI for implementation. My entire contribution was designing the guidelines and validating the result — the Input-Output loop that defines the engineer’s true value in this new model.
If you capture your thinking in the repo, the AI doesn’t just write code — it follows your logic.
This manual success was the catalyst for the custom commands I use today, and the proof that the framework works independently of any tooling.
The principle came first. The automation followed.
Making It Real: A Practical Implementation
The custom commands I built in Cursor and VS Code are an automation of the same discipline — backed by MCP servers for JIRA and Sonar that our organization already had in place.
Layer 1 — Intent
The workflow starts with /design-story
. You provide input — a screenshot, rough notes, a problem description, even a voice note. The command structures this into a proper user story with acceptance criteria, technical notes, and definition of done.
Critically, /create-story
queries JIRA via MCP, uses AI to match the story semantically to the most relevant existing epic, and creates the story in the right place. The story ID flows back into your IDE context — no tab switching, no manual copy-paste.
A well-crafted story is now a technical artifact, not just a PM communication. It is the specification that AI will execute against in Layer 2. That changes what good story writing means — precision and clarity in acceptance criteria are now engineering inputs, not just process compliance.
Layer 2 — Execution
When a sprint starts, /start-story or /assist-story [JIRA-ID]
fetches the full story from JIRA, loads acceptance criteria and technical context, sets up a feature branch with the org naming convention, and — this is the part that matters most — loads coding guidelines and architectural standards directly into the AI Agent’s context.
No more engineers copy-pasting guidelines from a wiki. No more AI-generated code that ignores your layering rules or naming conventions because they weren’t in scope. The standards are present from the first line.
The engineer’s role becomes that of a director rather than an implementer. The core question shifts from implementation to intent: ‘Is this the right solution?’ instead of ‘How do I write this?’ This is the ultimate application of engineering judgment.
Layer 3 — Validation
The /create-pr
command is where the framework proves its organizational value. Before a PR is even opened, it triggers an automated validation suite: checking commit messages against standards, verifying security and coverage via Sonar, and—most importantly—comparing the diff against the original story acceptance criteria to ensure the intent was met. It even validates code structure against our specific architectural patterns, such as Hexagonal Architecture and feature-level packaging.
The resulting PR description is generated from the story context and the diff summary — making it consistent, traceable, and reviewer-friendly. This is where AI enforces team standards that previously relied on individual discipline to maintain.
For the reviewer, /review-pr
runs these same checks, producing inline comments and a structured summary. The reviewer no longer just sees the code; they see the code in direct relation to the original intent.
What This Means for Engineering Teams
The best engineers on my team write the least code. They write the clearest problems.
If programming is increasingly delegated to AI, the engineers who thrive will be those who can define problems precisely, recognize when AI output is subtly wrong, make sound architectural tradeoffs, and communicate intent clearly enough that both humans and AI can execute against it.
That is a meaningfully different skill profile than what most engineering career ladders were built to reward. Coding proficiency, which was long the primary signal of engineering ability, is becoming table stakes rather than differentiation. The new differentiators are clarity of thought, quality of design, and the ability to direct AI toward the right outcome.
This shift has a quiet but significant implication for how teams estimate work. Historically, development effort was the anchor — design was 10% of that, testing 20%, and management 5%. Every other activity was calculated as a percentage of the largest number. But when AI compresses execution dramatically, that anchor no longer holds.
When AI compresses execution, the anchor shifts to intent. The effort that once went into writing code now needs to go into defining what the code should do — and verifying it did.
Teams that recognize this rebalance their investment accordingly — spending more time upfront on precise problem definition, and more time at the end on meaningful validation. The total effort may not change. Where it goes does.
The good news is that this shift is measurable — and it shows up clearly in the metrics that engineering organizations already use to evaluate team health. The DORA metrics — deployment frequency, lead time for change, change failure rate, and time to restore — all improve directly when this framework is applied:
▸ Deployment frequency increases because the pipeline from intent to validated PR compresses significantly. Less rework, fewer cycles.
▸ Lead time for change decreases because context is loaded once and carried through — no rebuilding mid-sprint, no guidelines hunted down mid-implementation.
▸ Change failure rate drops because the validation gate catches acceptance criteria gaps, security issues, and coverage failures before they reach production, not after.
▸ Time to restore improves because running documentation and traceable story-to-code links mean the team understands what changed and why — critical for fast incident response.
This framework doesn’t ask organizations to adopt new metrics or redefine success. It delivers better outcomes in the terms they already measure — and it does so by enabling engineers to spend more time on the work that actually creates value: understanding the problem, designing the right solution, deciding what to build next.
Closing Thought
Most teams using AI Agents today are capturing maybe 30% of the available value — the implementation acceleration. The remaining 70% sits in earlier and later stages: shaping intent precisely, keeping context alive across the workflow, and enforcing standards at the validation gate.
The shift left movement taught us that quality injected early is exponentially cheaper than quality enforced late. The same principle applies to AI. Inject it early. Keep it present. Let it enforce standards so engineers can focus on the work that actually requires human judgment — understanding the problem, designing the right solution, deciding what to build next.
That’s not a productivity argument. That’s a redefinition of what engineering excellence looks like
About the Author
Phani Krishna Vemireddy is an Engineer with 20+ years of enterprise systems experience. He leads the Sourcing Decision Engine platform at Walmart, working across sourcing optimization, cost engineering, and enterprise integration at scale.
